description: Framework-level evidence on validating an early idea before building. Coverage is framework-level; outcomes for any individual founder are highly variable.

items.title: Validate demand before building the product
items.content: A recurring failure mode for early founders is building before confirming that a specific person has a painful, frequent problem they will pay to solve. Customer-discovery practice recommends problem interviews and a falsifiable demand test before writing significant code. Framework-level guidance.
items.citation: Customer Development (Steve Blank); The Mom Test (Rob Fitzpatrick); YC startup guidance

items.title: Ask about past behavior, not hypothetical enthusiasm
items.content: Interview signals are noisy: people are polite and overstate hypothetical interest. The reliable signal is concrete past behavior — what they currently do, pay for, and work around — rather than 'would you use this?' Framework-level technique.

items.title: Startup outcomes have a wide, heavy-tailed distribution
items.content: Outcomes for new ventures are highly variable and heavy-tailed: most return little, a few return a lot. This is a base-rate framing, not a prediction about a specific idea or founder. The useful move is cheap experiments that update your odds, not estimating a personal probability.
items.citation: Venture outcome distributions (framework-level; aggregate, not individual)

items.title: Student-stage founding is often more reversible than it feels
items.content: For a student, a time-boxed validation sprint is frequently reversible: skills, network, and narrative often transfer back to employment tracks. The irreversible costs are usually opportunity cost (a missed recruiting cycle) and runway, not permanent lock-in. Framework-level.
items.citation: Reversible vs irreversible decision framing (one-way/two-way doors)

items.title: Co-founder fit and distribution are common bottlenecks
items.content: Beyond the idea, two frequent early bottlenecks are co-founder alignment and a repeatable way to reach users (distribution). Teams that can build but cannot reliably reach demand stall. Framework-level pattern.
